title: Event-Driven UI
user_familiarity: You're a data scientist with 10+ years of Python experience. You have heard of event-driven programming (perhaps from tkinter or matplotlib callbacks) but haven't studied it in the web UI context.
relevance_to_project: WellBegun's entire frontend is event-driven: user clicks, form submissions, and keyboard interactions trigger handler functions that call API endpoints and update reactive stores. Understanding events explains the chain from user action to UI update.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content_md:
Event-driven UI programming means your code mostly sits idle, waiting for user actions. When the user clicks, types, or submits a form, the browser generates an event and your registered handler function executes.

**Python Analogy:** Like registering callbacks in tkinter (`button.config(command=my_function)`) or matplotlib widgets. The framework is idle until the user acts, then your callback runs.

key_takeaways: User actions (click, type, submit) produce events that the browser dispatches to your code
Event handlers are callback functions registered to run when a specific event occurs
Events propagate through the DOM tree (bubbling), allowing parent elements to catch child events
In component frameworks like Svelte, the pattern is 'props down, events up'

sections.title: Events and Event Handlers
sections.content_md:
An event is a signal that something happened: click, submit, keypress. The browser creates an event object with details. An event handler is a function you attach to an element to run when that event occurs.

**Python Comparison:** Exactly like tkinter's `button.config(command=my_function)` or matplotlib's `button.on_clicked(my_function)`. You register a callback, and the framework calls it when the event occurs.

code_examples.annotation: In Svelte 5, event handlers are passed as properties using on<event> naming. When the user clicks, the browser fires a click event, and Svelte calls the handler.

sections.title: Common Event Types
sections.content_md:
Web browsers support dozens of event types. The most common in WellBegun: `click` (buttons, selection), `submit` (creating/updating entities), `input` (real-time filtering), `keydown` (keyboard shortcuts), `change` (dropdowns), `mouseenter`/`mouseleave` (tooltips, hover effects).

Each event carries an event object with properties like `event.target` (the element that fired it) and methods like `event.preventDefault()` (stop the browser's default behavior).

sections.title: Event Propagation (Bubbling)
sections.content_md:
When an event fires on an element, it bubbles up through the DOM tree. Clicking a `<button>` inside a `<div>` inside a `<form>` fires the click event on the button first, then the div, then the form, up to the document root.

This lets you attach a single handler to a parent element to catch events from all its children. You can stop propagation with `event.stopPropagation()`.

**Python Comparison:** Similar to exception handling where an exception propagates up the call stack until something catches it.

code_examples.code: click event path:  button -> div -> form -> body -> document
code_examples.annotation: Events bubble from the target element up through every ancestor to the document root.

sections.title: Component Communication: Props Down, Events Up
sections.content_md:
In component-based frameworks like Svelte: parents pass data to children as props, children notify parents by calling event callbacks. The child doesn't know how deletion works -- it just notifies the parent.

**Python Comparison:** Like the Strategy pattern in OOP -- the parent injects behavior (the delete function) into the child. The child calls the function without knowing its implementation.

code_examples.code:
handleDelete(myNote.id)} />

{note.title}
code_examples.annotation: Parent passes note data down (props) and a callback. Child calls the callback when delete is clicked (events up). The child doesn't know about APIs or stores.

sections.title: Form Submission Events
sections.content_md: Forms are a special case. When submitted, the browser fires a `submit` event. The `e.preventDefault()` call is critical -- without it, the browser reloads the page (default form behavior from the 1990s). In a modern single-page app like WellBegun, you handle submission in JavaScript and update the UI reactively.

code_examples.code:
<form onsubmit={(e) => {
    e.preventDefault();
    createNote({ title, content });
}}>
    <input bind:value={title} />
    <textarea bind:value={content}></textarea>
    <button type="submit">Create Note</button>
</form>
code_examples.annotation: preventDefault() stops the browser from reloading the page. The handler calls the API function instead, keeping the single-page app intact.

sections.title: In This Project
sections.content_md:
WellBegun uses event-driven patterns throughout: entity creation forms (submit -> API POST -> store refresh), graph interactions (click on nodes -> select entity -> open detail panel), panel/tab navigation (click -> update store).

**The full event chain for creating a note:**
1. User types in form fields (input events update local state via `bind:value`)
2. User clicks 'Create' (submit event fires)
3. Handler calls `e.preventDefault()`
4. Handler calls `createNote({ title, content })` (API POST request)
5. Backend creates the note and returns it
6. Handler calls `loadNotes()` to refresh the store
7. `notes.set(newData)` notifies subscribers
8. All components using `$notes` re-render

This chain connects three concepts: events (user action), client-server communication (API call), and reactivity (store update triggers re-render).

sections.title: The Full Event Chain — Creating a Note
sections.content_md:
Trace the complete chain from user click to UI update:

**Step 1:** User clicks submit. Browser fires `submit` event on the form.
**Step 2:** Handler calls `e.preventDefault()` then `createNote({ title, content })`.
**Step 3:** `createNote()` sends a POST request to the backend API.
**Step 4:** After success, `loadNotes()` refreshes the store.
**Step 5:** Every component using `$notes` re-renders with the new list.

The chain: **user click -> submit event -> preventDefault -> API POST -> store refresh -> reactive UI update**.

guided_steps.description: User clicks submit button. Browser fires submit event on the form.
Event handler runs: preventDefault() stops page reload, then calls API function
createNote() sends POST request to backend
loadNotes() refreshes the store with fresh data
All components using $notes re-render with the updated list

metadata.key_insight: The chain connects three concepts: events (user action), client-server communication (API call), and reactivity (store update triggers re-render).

sections.title: Props Down, Events Up — Component Communication
sections.content_md:
A parent component renders a list of notes. Each NoteCard has a delete button. The parent passes the note data down as a prop and a delete callback. The child calls the callback when delete is clicked. The child has no idea about API calls or store refreshes — it just calls the function the parent provided.

Data flows **down** (parent passes `note` prop). Events flow **up** (child calls `ondelete` callback). This separation of concerns keeps components simple and reusable.

metadata.key_insight: Props down, events up creates a clear, predictable data flow. Children are reusable because they don't depend on specific parent implementations.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.content_md:
### Exercise 1: Trace the Event Flow

A user clicks a 'Delete' button inside a NoteCard component. NoteCard is inside NoteList, which is inside App. Describe the event flow: which component handles the click? How does the parent know the note was deleted?

### Exercise 2: Why preventDefault()?

What would happen if you removed `e.preventDefault()` from a form's submit handler? Why is it necessary in a single-page app?

### Exercise 3: Design an Event Chain

Design the event chain for editing an existing note. From 'Save' click through API call to UI re-render, list every step.

exercises.title: Trace the Event Flow
exercises.task: A user clicks 'Delete' inside a NoteCard. NoteCard is inside NoteList, inside App. Describe the event flow.
exercises.hint: The click fires on the button in NoteCard. NoteCard's handler calls the ondelete callback prop provided by the parent. The parent's callback calls the API and refreshes the store.
exercises.expected_answer: Click event fires on button -> NoteCard calls ondelete callback -> Parent's handleDelete runs -> API deleteNote() called -> loadNotes() refreshes store -> all $notes subscribers re-render.

exercises.title: Why preventDefault()?
exercises.task: What happens without e.preventDefault() in a form submit handler?
exercises.hint: The browser's default behavior is to submit the form via a full page navigation (GET or POST), which would reload the page and destroy all client-side state.
exercises.expected_answer: Without preventDefault(), the browser reloads the page, destroying all client-side state (stores, component state). In a single-page app, form submission is handled in JavaScript instead.

exercises.title: Design an Event Chain
exercises.task: Design the event chain for editing an existing note, from Save click to UI re-render.
exercises.hint: Submit event -> preventDefault -> updateNote(id, data) API call -> loadNotes() store refresh -> reactive re-render.
exercises.expected_answer: (1) Submit event, (2) preventDefault, (3) updateNote(id, {title, content}) sends PUT/PATCH, (4) Backend updates note, (5) loadNotes() refreshes store, (6) notes.set(newData) notifies subscribers, (7) All $notes components re-render.

sections.title: Knowledge Check
sections.content_md: Test your understanding of event-driven UI programming concepts.

questions.question: What is an event in web UI programming?
options: A scheduled task that runs at a specific time
A signal that something happened (user click, key press, form submit) that triggers registered handler functions
A message sent from the server to the client
A CSS animation trigger
questions.explanation: Events are the browser's way of telling your code that something happened. The browser creates an event object with details and dispatches it to registered handlers.

questions.question: What does e.preventDefault() do in a form submit handler?
options: Prevents the form from being displayed
Prevents other event handlers from running
Stops the browser's default behavior (page reload on form submit) so you can handle it in JavaScript
Prevents the event from bubbling up
questions.explanation: By default, submitting a form causes a full page navigation. In a single-page app, you want to handle submission with JavaScript (API call + store update) instead.

questions.question: What does 'props down, events up' mean?
options: Data is stored in props, events are stored in the DOM
Parent components pass data to children via props, and children notify parents by calling event callbacks
Props flow from the server down, events flow from the client up
Events must always be defined before props
questions.explanation: The parent passes data (like a note object) down as a prop. The child notifies the parent when something happens (like a delete click) by calling a callback function the parent provided.

questions.question: What is event bubbling?
options: Events fire multiple times on the same element
Events propagate from the target element up through its ancestor elements in the DOM tree
Events are delayed until the next render cycle
Events are converted to store updates automatically
questions.explanation: When you click a button inside a div inside a form, the click event fires on the button first, then bubbles up to the div, then the form, and so on up to the document root.

questions.question: In WellBegun, what is the complete event chain when a user creates a note via a form?
options: Click -> page reload -> database update
Submit event -> preventDefault -> API POST request -> store refresh (loadNotes) -> reactive UI update
Click -> direct DOM update -> database sync
Submit event -> store update -> API call
questions.explanation: The form submit fires, preventDefault stops the page reload, the API function sends a POST request, then loadNotes() refreshes the store, and all subscribed components re-render.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.content_md: Curated resources for deepening your understanding of event-driven UI programming.
